text:
St Mary's Hospital

Institute of Neurology

Clinic Date: 14 October 2025

Dr Patel
St Mary's Hospital
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY

Dear Dr Patel,

Emily Carter, DOB: 17-08-1989, Hospital No: C672154 NHS No. 4728395612
78 Willow Lane, London, W9 3HT

Medication:

Levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily; Clobazam 10 mg nocte as required for clusters.

Thank you for asking me to review Emily Carter. She reports that her work as a rotating shift worker, including regular night shifts, has significantly disrupted her sleep-wake cycle. She describes irregular sleep duration, frequent early starts after late finishes, and difficulty maintaining a consistent circadian rhythm. She has been attempting sleep hygiene strategies (dark room, avoiding screens pre-bed, planned naps before nights) and uses a shift rota app, though with limited success due to unpredictable cover shifts.

There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. She has one to two absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment. She attributes better short-term control to more regular sleep over the past fortnight while on day shifts, and she has kept a seizure and sleep diary which supports this temporal association. She denies new focal neurological symptoms, injury, or prolonged post-ictal confusion beyond her usual pattern. There is no history of status epilepticus.

She reports good adherence with levetiracetam, missing approximately one dose per month, typically on transition days between shifts. She has used clobazam on two occasions for brief clusters with perceived benefit and without troublesome sedation. No adverse drug reactions are reported. Alcohol is minimal; she avoids caffeine after midday on day shifts but uses it overnight on nights. There are no intercurrent illnesses and pregnancy is not planned.

On examination today, observations were stable and neurological examination was unremarkable. We discussed practical measures to mitigate sleep loss around shifts, including pre-planned naps prior to nights, strategic light exposure, and consistent timing of morning doses irrespective of shift pattern, which she has agreed to continue. She does not currently drive and understands DVLA guidance regarding her seizure pattern. A workplace occupational health review has been suggested by the patient to explore rota adjustments; she will pursue this with her employer.

Plan:
- Continue levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily.
- Clobazam 10 mg nocte as needed for brief clusters, not exceeding three consecutive nights; patient to record usage in diary.
- Maintain detailed seizure and sleep diary; bring to next review.
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D.
- Consider melatonin as a sleep aid if diary suggests persistent circadian misalignment despite behavioural strategies; to be discussed at next visit.

We will review her in three months, sooner if she experiences any increase in events or adverse effects.

Yours sincerely,

She has one to two absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned: (1) "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly" → 1 to 2 per week. (2) "She has one to two absences per day" → 1 to 2 per day. (3) "clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month" → clusters monthly but not higher frequency than daily absences. Rule: choose the highest frequency across types; daily absences (1 to 2 per day) are more frequent than weekly GTCs and monthly clusters. Note: The sentence "No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment" likely refers to the short interval since last visit and does not meet 6-month seizure-free criterion, so not seizure free. Therefore, final label is 1 to 2 per day. Provide verbatim quote supporting the chosen frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: She has one to two absences per day.
reference: There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. She has one to two absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment
